1: Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter the sheep of my pasture! declares YHWH. Chapter 23 opens with a comprehensive indictment of Judah's leadership under the shepherd metaphor that was ubiquitous in ancient Near Eastern royal ideology. In Mesopotamian tradition, the king as shepherd (re'û in Akkadian) was one of the primary royal titles — the king who gathers and cares for the people-flock. Jeremiah's woe reverses this: the shepherds of Israel have been the destroyers and scatterers rather than the gatherers and protectors.
2: Therefore thus says YHWH, the God of Israel, concerning the shepherds who care for my people: You have scattered my flock and have driven them away, and you have not attended to them. Behold, I will attend to you for your evil deeds, declares YHWH. The covenant recompense formula is precise: they failed to attend to (paqad) the flock; YHWH will attend to them — but as judge rather than shepherd. The same verb paqad that means caring visitation when YHWH acts on behalf of his people (Exodus 3:16; Ruth 1:6) becomes the verb of judicial visitation when directed against covenant violators.
3: Then I will gather the remnant of my flock out of all the countries where I have driven them, and I will bring them back to their fold, and they shall be fruitful and multiply. The restoration promise follows immediately on the judgment: YHWH himself will become the shepherd where the human shepherds failed. The fruitful and multiply (paru verabu) formula is the creation blessing (Genesis 1:28) applied to the restored remnant — a new-creation community gathered from the nations. The ingathering from all the countries is broader than the Babylonian exile, anticipating a universal diaspora restoration.
4: I will set shepherds over them who will care for them, and they shall fear no more, nor be dismayed, neither shall any be missing, declares YHWH. New, faithful shepherds are promised — human leaders who will perform the function the current leaders have abandoned. None shall be missing (velo-yippaqedu) — the same paqad verb again: none will be unaccounted for. The restored flock will be fully complete, not scattered and lost as at present. This anticipates the Good Shepherd discourse of John 10 (I lay down my life for the sheep; I know my own and my own know me; I lose none of all that the Father has given me — John 10:28–29).
5: Behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch, and he shall reign as king and deal wisely, and shall execute justice and righteousness in the land. The Righteous Branch (tzemach tsaddiq) oracle is one of the most important messianic passages in the Hebrew Bible. The Branch (tzemach) imagery for the Davidic Messiah appears also in Isaiah 11:1 (a shoot from the stump of Jesse), Zechariah 3:8 and Jeremiah 6:12 (my servant the Branch), and anticipates NT titles for Jesus as the shoot from David's root (Revelation 22:16). Deal wisely (hiskil) — the wisdom-king of Isaiah 11:2 and the sapient ideal of Solomonic literature. Justice and righteousness: the paired terms of vv3 and 5 form the defining marks of the messianic reign, contrasting with the failed justice of the current kings.
6: In his days Judah will be saved, and Israel will dwell securely. And this is the name by which he will be called: The LORD Is Our Righteousness. YHWH Tsidkenu — The LORD is Our Righteousness — is the messianic king's throne-name. It is a pointed wordplay on the name of the reigning king Zedekiah (Tzidqiyahu = YHWH is my righteousness), who was righteous in name only and whose kingdom was collapsing. The true king who will come will actually embody the righteousness that Zedekiah's name promised but his life denied. Paul picks up this theme: Christ has become for us righteousness (1 Corinthians 1:30); we become the righteousness of God in him (2 Corinthians 5:21).
7: Therefore, behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when they shall no longer say, As YHWH lives who brought up the people of Israel out of the land of Egypt. The new-Exodus motif from Jeremiah 16:14–15 reappears: the Messiah's reign will inaugurate a new saving act so great that it supersedes the Exodus as Israel's primary confession of divine deliverance. The repetition of this theme (Jeremiah 16:14–15 and Jeremiah 23:7–8) signals that it is a central element of Jeremiah's eschatological hope — not replacement of the Exodus memory but its fulfillment in a grander act of divine redemption.
8: But as YHWH lives who brought up and led the offspring of the house of Israel out of the north country and out of all the countries where he had driven them. Then they shall dwell in their own land. The new Exodus is from the north country — Babylon — and from all nations. The offspring of the house of Israel is broader than Judah: the promise encompasses the reunification of the divided kingdoms, all twelve tribes gathered into the covenant land. The NT reads this through the lens of Christ's gathering of the scattered children of God from all nations (John 11:52).
9: Concerning the prophets: My heart is broken within me; all my bones shake; I am like a drunken man, like a man overcome by wine, because of YHWH and because of his holy words. The oracle against false prophets (vv9–40) begins with Jeremiah's physical response to what he is about to say. The prophet's body is convulsed by the message before he delivers it — a mark of authentic prophetic experience. Because of YHWH and because of his holy words: the holiness of the divine word makes its violation — by false prophets who claim to speak it — a cause of existential anguish for anyone who has genuinely received it.
10: For the land is full of adulterers; because of the curse the land mourns, and the pastures of the wilderness are dried up. Their course is evil, and their might is not right. The land's suffering (drought, dried pastures) is linked to spiritual adultery — the metaphor for idolatry and covenant infidelity that runs throughout the prophets (Hosea, Ezekiel 16). The curse (alah) — the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28 — has already taken effect in the landscape. The prophets' course is evil and their might is not right: they are vigorous in the wrong direction.
11: Both prophet and priest are ungodly; even in my house I have found their evil, declares YHWH. The combined indictment of prophet and priest (cf. Jeremiah 2:8; Jeremiah 6:13; Jeremiah 8:10) in the Temple itself — the most sacred space — is the worst possible location for religious corruption. Evil in YHWH's house is a direct desecration of the covenant relationship. The Temple, supposed to be the place of divine encounter and covenant maintenance, has become the location of institutionalized spiritual corruption.
12: Therefore their way shall be to them like slippery paths in the darkness, into which they shall be driven and fall, for I will bring disaster upon them in the year of their punishment, declares YHWH. The slippery paths in darkness are the paths of those who walk without YHWH's light — the false prophets who lead people into darkness will themselves find no footing. The year of their punishment (shenat pequdatam) is the appointed time of covenant reckoning (cf. Jeremiah 11:23; Jeremiah 46:21; Jeremiah 50:27).
13: In the prophets of Samaria I saw an unsavory thing: they prophesied by Baal and led my people Israel astray. The prophets of the northern kingdom (destroyed by Assyria in 722 BC) prophesied by Baal — explicit pagan prophecy. This is presented as unsavory (tiphel, tasteless, disgusting) but at least consistent — they were openly aligned with Baal-worship.
14: But in the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen a horrible thing: they commit adultery and walk in lies; they strengthen the hands of evildoers, so that no one turns from his evil; all of them have become like Sodom to me, and its inhabitants like Gomorrah. Jerusalem's prophets are worse than Samaria's: they commit adultery (literal and metaphorical covenant infidelity), walk in lies (false prophecy), and — most damagingly — strengthen the hands of evildoers. False prophecy is not merely error; it actively prevents repentance by telling people what they want to hear. Like Sodom… like Gomorrah: the paradigmatic examples of divine judgment for moral corruption. Jerusalem's prophets have brought the city to Sodom's moral level.
15: Therefore thus says YHWH of hosts concerning the prophets: Behold, I will feed them with bitter food and give them poisoned water to drink, for from the prophets of Jerusalem ungodliness has gone out into all the land. The prophets' food and water — wormwood and poisoned water — are the covenant curses turned inward. The spreading of ungodliness (chanuppah, profaneness, pollution) from the capital outward into the entire nation makes the prophets instruments of national corruption. False prophecy is an epidemic disease spread through the religious system.
16: Thus says YHWH of hosts: Do not listen to the words of the prophets who prophesy to you, filling you with vain hopes. They speak visions of their own minds, not from the mouth of YHWH. The criterion for false prophecy: visions of their own minds (chazon libam yedabberu, they speak the vision of their heart). The authentic prophetic word originates with YHWH and passes through the prophet; false prophecy originates with the prophet's own desires and preferences and is then falsely attributed to YHWH. Filling you with vain hopes (mehavlim etkhem — making you hevel, vapor, vapor-filling you): the false prophets produce the illusion of security with no substance behind it.
17: They say continually to those who despise the word of YHWH, It shall be well with you; and to everyone who stubbornly follows his own heart, they say, No disaster shall come upon you. The specific content of false prophecy: unconditional shalom (peace, well-being) for those who despise YHWH's word and follow their own stubborn hearts. This is the pastoral malpractice that Jeremiah has condemned since Jeremiah 6:14 (peace, peace, when there is no peace). False prophecy enables wickedness by removing the covenant consequences. It is the prophetic equivalent of grace that allows sin to continue (cf. Romans 6:1).
18: For who among them has stood in the council of YHWH to see and to hear his word, or who has paid attention to his word and listened? The divine council (sod YHWH) is the heavenly assembly where YHWH deliberates and makes decisions, attended by the heavenly beings (cf. 1 Kings 22:19–23; Isaiah 6:1–8; Job 1–2; Psalms 82:1). The prophetic claim to authority was grounded in actual access to this council — the prophet is YHWH's messenger from the divine assembly. Amos 3:7 states the principle: YHWH does nothing without revealing his secret (sod) to his servants the prophets. The false prophets have never had this access; they speak without council authorization.
19: Behold, the storm of YHWH! Wrath has gone forth, a whirling tempest; it will burst upon the head of the wicked. The storm-theophany announces the divine judgment that the false prophets denied would come. The whirling tempest (sa'ar mitchollel) is both cosmic imagery and specific historical event — the Babylonian army advancing like a divine storm. The false prophets said no disaster shall come (v17); the divine storm is already in motion.
20: The anger of YHWH will not turn back until he has executed and accomplished the intents of his heart. In the latter days you will understand it clearly. The judgment is irreversible until it has run its course. In the latter days you will understand it clearly (be'acharit hayamim titbonenu bah binah): the people who are currently refusing to believe Jeremiah's message will understand it when events unfold. Historical fulfillment will produce the theological clarity that prophetic announcement could not. This is not vindictive but pastoral: the understanding will come, even if too late to prevent the judgment.
21: I did not send the prophets, yet they ran; I did not speak to them, yet they prophesied. The two criteria of authentic prophecy restated negatively: I did not send (cf. Jeremiah 14:14 — the sent/not-sent criterion of Deuteronomy 18:20) and I did not speak to them. The false prophets' activity has all the outward form of prophecy — running, prophesying, delivering oracles — without the divine commissioning or content. It is form without substance, performance without divine backing.
22: But if they had stood in my council, then they would have proclaimed my words to the people, and they would have turned them from their evil way, and from the evil of their deeds. The counterfactual: had the false prophets genuinely stood in YHWH's council, they would have proclaimed the actual divine word — which would have produced repentance. The test of true prophecy is ultimately its fruit: a message that enables people to turn from evil is more likely to be from YHWH than one that confirms them in it. The fruitlessness of the false prophets' ministry is itself evidence of its inauthenticity.
23: Am I a God at hand, declares YHWH, and not a God far away? One of Jeremiah's most theologically significant statements on divine omnipresence. The question anticipates the assumption of the false prophets: that YHWH can be treated as a local deity (elohim mikarov, a nearby god) whose activity is spatially limited. If YHWH were merely a local temple-deity, the false prophets could claim access to his immediate presence while he remained ignorant of their actual standing in the heavenly council.
24: Can a man hide himself in secret places so that I cannot see him? declares YHWH. Do I not fill heaven and earth? declares YHWH. The divine omnipresence is asserted as the basis for divine omniscience: I fill heaven and earth (et-hashamayim ve'et ha'aretz ani male'). There is no hiding place. The false prophets who deceive the people cannot deceive YHWH — he sees the vision of their own hearts (v16) for what it is. The omnipresence argument defeats the false prophets' possibility of operating in YHWH's name without YHWH's knowledge.
25: I have heard what the prophets have said who prophesy lies in my name, saying, I have dreamed, I have dreamed! Dreams as prophetic vehicles were standard in the ancient Near East — Egyptian dream interpretation was a royal science; Mesopotamian dream-omens filled omen-text collections. Dreams were a legitimate medium of divine communication in Israel (Genesis 28:12; Jeremiah 37:5–11; 1 Kings 3:5). The false prophets exploit this legitimate form: I have dreamed is a claim to divine revelation through the standard channel, but the content is self-generated.
26: How long shall there be lies in the heart of the prophets who prophesy lies, and who prophesy the deceit of their own heart? The question how long? (ad matay) presses toward the end of the false-prophecy epidemic. The heart is the source of both true prophecy (YHWH writes on hearts, Jeremiah 31:33) and false prophecy (the prophets speak from their own hearts, v16). The difference is whether the heart has been shaped by the divine word or has merely generated its own desires and labeled them divine.
27: Who think to make my people forget my name by their dreams that they tell one another, even as their fathers forgot my name for Baal. The false prophets' dreams function in exactly the same way as Baal worship: they cause the people to forget YHWH's name (lishekoach et-shemi). The goal of authentic prophecy is to keep YHWH's name in the forefront of national consciousness; the effect of false prophecy is name-forgetting — the same apostasy that the entire book of Jeremiah is diagnosing.
28: Let the prophet who has a dream tell the dream, but let him who has my word speak my word faithfully. What has straw in common with wheat? declares YHWH. The straw-versus-wheat contrast is the criterion of prophetic content. Wheat is substantive, nourishing, real; straw is the chaff left after threshing — light, worthless, blown away. Dream-prophecy and the genuine word of YHWH are as different as straw and wheat. Jeremiah does not forbid dream-prophecy but relativizes it: let those who have dreams tell them, but they should not be confused with the actual divine word.
29: Is not my word like fire, declares YHWH, and like a hammer that breaks the rock in pieces? Two images for the power of the genuine divine word: fire that burns and consumes (cf. Jeremiah 20:9 — the burning fire shut up in Jeremiah's bones), and a hammer that breaks rock. The word of YHWH is not a gentle suggestion but an irresistible force that shatters even the hardest resistance. The contrast with dream-prophecy (straw, v28) could not be more stark: straw drifts in the wind; the hammer word breaks rock.
30: Therefore, behold, I am against the prophets, declares YHWH, who steal my words from one another. The false prophets plagiarize — they steal divine words (ganvim devari) from each other. The charge of stealing could mean appropriating authentic prophetic oracles and falsely applying them, or recycling each other's fabricated oracles as if they were divine revelation. The communal circulation of false prophecy creates an echo-chamber effect: each prophet reinforces the others, producing an illusion of corroborating testimony.
31: Behold, I am against the prophets, declares YHWH, who use their tongues and declare, declares the Lord. The formula ne'um YHWH (declares the LORD / oracle of YHWH) appears hundreds of times in the prophetic books as the stamp of divine authority. The false prophets have mastered the formula without having received the divine word it is supposed to authenticate. The form of prophetic authority (declares the LORD) is used to give false content the appearance of divine backing.
32: Behold, I am against those who prophesy lying dreams, declares YHWH, and who tell them and lead my people astray by their lies and their recklessness, when I did not send them or charge them. So they do not profit this people at all, declares YHWH. The triple indictment of false prophets — lying dreams, recklessness, unsent — concludes with the practical criterion: they do not profit this people at all. The test of prophetic ministry is its actual benefit to the community. False prophecy, whatever its form, produces no genuine spiritual profit because it leads people away from the covenant relationship that alone provides real benefit.
33: When one of this people, or a prophet or a priest asks you, What is the burden of YHWH? you shall say to them, You are the burden, and I will cast you off, declares YHWH. The massa oracle (vv33–40) is a complex wordplay. Massa means both burden and oracle/prophetic pronouncement. The false prophets have been using massa as a standard designation for their utterances — the oracle of YHWH. Jeremiah turns the word against them: you are the burden. The double meaning creates a devastating pun: the people who keep asking for YHWH's oracle are themselves the burden YHWH will cast off.
34: And as for the prophet, priest, or one of the people who says, The burden of YHWH, I will punish that man and his household. The prohibition on using massa (burden/oracle) for prophetic utterances is radical: YHWH is banning the standard prophetic formula because it has been so thoroughly corrupted. The corruption of religious language — using the right words to convey wrong content — is treated as a capital-level offense, extending punishment to the entire household.
35: Thus shall you say, every one to his neighbor and every one to his brother: What has YHWH answered? or What has YHWH spoken? An alternative formula is provided: instead of what is the burden of YHWH? (the corrupted formula), the people should ask what has YHWH answered? What has YHWH spoken? The distinction is subtle but real: the new formula emphasizes receptivity (what has he answered/spoken?) rather than a title for the prophetic genre (burden). It positions the speaker as recipient rather than authoritative deliverer.
36: But the burden of YHWH you shall mention no more, for the burden is every man's own word, and you pervert the words of the living God, YHWH of hosts, our God. The ban on massa: the formula has been so colonized by false prophecy that it now means the opposite of what it should — every man's own word masquerading as divine speech. To use it is to pervert the words of the living God (vatehafechu et-divrei Elohim chayyim). The divine title living God (Elohim chayyim) — the God who is actually alive, who actually speaks — stands against the dead idols and their fraudulent prophets.
37: Thus you shall say to the prophet: What has YHWH answered you? or What has YHWH spoken? The alternative formula is to be used even when addressing prophets. The prophet is not exempt from the standard of honest reporting — a genuine prophet will have an actual divine answer to report. The question puts the prophet on notice that YHWH's actual speaking (or non-speaking) is the real question, not the form of the prophetic delivery.
38: But if you say, The burden of YHWH, thus says YHWH: Because you have said these words, The burden of YHWH, when I sent to you, saying, You shall not say, The burden of YHWH. The judgment for continued use of the banned formula is severe: YHWH has explicitly commanded against it; to continue using it is deliberate disobedience of a direct divine command. The severity of the coming punishment matches the deliberateness of the violation.
39: Therefore, behold, I will surely lift you up and cast you away from my presence, you and the city that I gave to you and your fathers. The punishment for false prophecy and the perversion of divine speech is total: lifting up and casting away from YHWH's presence. The verb nasa (lift/carry away) continues the massa wordplay: you wanted to carry YHWH's burden-oracle; YHWH will carry you — to exile. The city given to them and their fathers will also be taken away: the covenant gift is revoked.
40: And I will bring upon you everlasting reproach and perpetual shame, that will not be forgotten. The chapter ends with the permanent shaming of false prophets: everlasting reproach and perpetual shame (cherpat olam ubushat olam). In the honor-shame culture of the ancient Near East, permanent shame was the worst possible social fate — the destruction of one's name and legacy. The false prophets who sought honor through claiming divine speech will receive everlasting dishonor. Their names will be remembered — but as bywords for prophetic fraud.
